title: The Secret Code

jokes.text: We're chickens in an experiment...waiting for that pellet to come down the shoot.
jokes.setup: Jerry sets up a bit about ATM machines and how people congregate around them.
jokes.punchline: Comparing ATM users to Pavlovian chickens waiting for a pellet — reducing modern banking behavior to animal conditioning.
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational premise with a vivid, original comparison. The Pavlovian chicken image is genuinely funny and apt. Solid stand-up material, though not transcendent.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever notice how people act at the ATM machine? They're like lab rats waiting for a food pellet. You push the button, they stand there staring at the slot like something magical is about to happen. And when the money comes out, they grab it like they just won the lottery. 'Oh! Oh! It worked!' No, it's supposed to work! That's what the machine does!

jokes.text: You're waiting for the sound. That's what we're trained to hear. The here-comes-the-money sound.
jokes.setup: The ATM-as-Pavlovian-experiment premise continues.
jokes.punchline: Naming the specific auditory reward — 'the here-comes-the-money sound' — with total earnestness pinpoints the absurdity of conditioned banking behavior.
jokes.explanation: Builds nicely on the prior premise. The informal coinage 'here-comes-the-money sound' is economical and funny. Strong continuation of the bit.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what's amazing? People get excited about the ATM. They really do. It's like a magical money machine. You put your card in, you punch some numbers, and MONEY comes out! And people treat it like a slot machine that actually pays off.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm serious, watch people at the ATM. They get giddy. 'Oh! Oh! It's giving me money! Look at that! It's actually WORKING!' Like they're shocked every single time. As if they thought, 'Well, maybe today it'll just keep my card and laugh at me.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And the beeping! Why does it beep? To congratulate you? 'Congratulations! You've successfully remembered your PIN! Here's your money!'

jokes.text: 'It's coming. It's coming. They're giving me money.'
jokes.setup: Jerry has established ATM users as conditioned animals; now he voices the internal monologue.
jokes.punchline: The childlike, almost disbelieving inner voice ('They're giving me money') captures the absurd joy of receiving your own money, said as if it's a gift.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the stand-up bit. Voicing the irrational internal monologue ('They're giving me money' — it's YOUR money) is the sharpest observation in the ATM bit. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The bank says never tell anyone your PIN number. It's for security.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what, you're taking banking advice on relationships now? What's next, you gonna tell her to keep her assets diversified?

jokes.text: It says very clearly, 'For your protection...do not give your secret code to anyone.' So you're taking relationship advice from Chemical Bank now?
jokes.setup: George refuses to give his fiancée Susan his ATM code, citing the bank's printed instructions.
jokes.punchline: Jerry mock-credulous reaction: 'So you're taking relationship advice from Chemical Bank now?' — reframing George's literalism as absurd deference to a corporation.
jokes.explanation: This is the thesis joke of the episode and it lands beautifully. The reframe — taking bank boilerplate as relationship philosophy — is sharp, economical, and perfectly suited to Jerry's deadpan wit. It also nails George's particular brand of irrational rigidity. Quotable and structurally clean: the setup builds across multiple lines and the punchline lands in one tight sentence.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What's wrong with wanting a little privacy? Is that selfish?

jokes.text: Why does everything have to be 'us'? Is there no 'me' left? Why can't there be some things just for me? Is that so selfish?
jokes.setup: The argument is about an ATM code, a trivial secret.
jokes.punchline: George escalates the stakes to an existential crisis about selfhood — over a bank PIN.
jokes.explanation: Pure George — catastrophizing the mundane into philosophy. Deeply character-specific. The escalation from 'ATM code' to existential identity is classic Costanza logic.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Isn't that a little selfish?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Of course it's selfish. That's the whole point.

jokes.text: Actually, that's the definition of selfish.
jokes.setup: George asks rhetorically 'Is that so selfish?' fully expecting sympathy.
jokes.punchline: Jerry confirms, flatly and literally, that yes — wanting things just for yourself is the definition of selfish.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — four words do all the work. Jerry's literal deflation of George's rhetorical question is a perfectly timed straight-man beat. The joke is 100% in the delivery's matter-of-fact quality. Structurally impeccable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You're not gonna give me your ATM code.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not? Here it is: 1939.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: 1939? That's the year Superman was created.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So? You've gotta give me something a little less predictable than that.

jokes.text: No one's ever asked. Do you want it? It's Jor-El.
jokes.setup: George asks if Jerry has given his ATM code to anyone, implying it's a universal intimacy threshold.
jokes.punchline: Jerry casually volunteers his own code — 'Jor-El' — before anyone even asked, undercutting George's drama entirely.
jokes.explanation: Double-punch: Jerry deflates George's precious secrecy by being totally indifferent about his own code, AND the code itself is 'Jor-El' — Superman's father — which is perfectly Jerry, geeky and self-amused. Both the move and the content land.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're saying you'd never break the code?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's right. I live by a code.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Even if your life depended on it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, no, if my life depended on it. But also if there was a significant cash prize involved.

jokes.text: Superman's father on Krypton. Of course.
jokes.setup: Jerry reveals his ATM code is 'Jor-El.'
jokes.punchline: George's instant, resigned 'of course' — as if this is the most predictably Jerry thing Jerry has ever done.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly timed reaction beat. 'Of course' is doing enormous work — it's both an explanation for the audience and a character reveal about how well George knows Jerry. Economy is outstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You see, when you sleep, you're not supposed to cross the same leg every night. You gotta alternate. I forgot to alternate.

jokes.text: What's the matter with your leg? My foot fell asleep. How did your foot fall asleep? I crossed my legs. I forgot to alternate.
jokes.setup: George is sitting with his leg crossed when Fred arrives.
jokes.punchline: 'I forgot to alternate' — as if there's a systematic leg-crossing protocol George routinely fails to maintain.
jokes.explanation: Great small joke. 'I forgot to alternate' is a perfect George answer — hyper-specific, pseudoscientific self-explanation that implies a whole ridiculous leg-crossing routine. Sets up the recurring foot-asleep gag too.

dialogue.speaker: Fred
dialogue.line: Your foot fell asleep? I'm so jealous. My foot hasn't slept in years.

jokes.text: You're lucky. At least you got something to do.
jokes.setup: Fred is told George's foot fell asleep. Fred responds with genuine envy.
jokes.punchline: Fred, apparently so profoundly bored with his life, envies a man whose foot fell asleep — the mildest possible thing to envy.
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan character introduction. Fred is immediately established as profoundly disengaged from life — the gap between 'something to do' and 'foot fell asleep' is very funny. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He doesn't remember me? We met at that party!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Maybe you didn't make an impression.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I didn't make an impression? I was hilarious! I was charming! I was... I was there!

jokes.text: Fred says 'Nice to meet you' to Elaine, then immediately leaves — but they've met before, and Elaine is visibly offended.
jokes.setup: Elaine is introduced to Fred.
jokes.punchline: Fred says 'Nice to meet you' and leaves promptly. Elaine realizes he doesn't remember her from a previous meeting — his social dismissal is casual and total.
jokes.explanation: A relatable social cringe scenario executed efficiently. The joke is in the gap between Fred's breezy pleasantry and Elaine's dawning realization of the insult. Sets up her subplot effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You don't remember? We were talking about where you were when JFK was assassinated.

dialogue.speaker: Fred
dialogue.line: I have no idea what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Come on! You told me this whole crazy story. You said you were at a parade, and there were pigeons everywhere, and you were eating a hot dog...

dialogue.speaker: Fred
dialogue.line: That doesn't sound like me at all.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: And then you said the hot dog fell out of your hand, and a pigeon ate it, and you chased the pigeon for three blocks!

jokes.text: And he didn't remember you? [beat] Where are you going? I gotta go talk to him.
jokes.setup: Elaine is upset that Fred didn't remember meeting her at a party; Jerry asks 'And he didn't remember you?' with the implicit disbelief.
jokes.punchline: Instead of commiserating, Elaine immediately decides to confront Fred directly — the absurdly aggressive response to a minor social slight.
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat — Elaine's impulsive decision to confront someone immediately is very much her. The humor is mostly situational and behavioral rather than verbal.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You don't remember me? We talked about this!

dialogue.speaker: Fred
dialogue.line: I don't recall that conversation at all.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: How can you not remember? We just discussed it!

dialogue.speaker: Fred
dialogue.line: Well, I do remember the bathroom door at the coffee shop had that graffiti that said 'For a good time call Jennifer' with a heart around it, and someone had written 'Jennifer is a liar' underneath in blue pen, and there was a small doodle of a smiley face in the corner with one eye bigger than the other.

jokes.text: I talked about how my uncle worked in the book depository building with Lee Harvey Oswald. When my uncle said to him, 'The president's been shot,' Oswald winked at him and said, 'I'm gonna go catch a movie.'
jokes.setup: Elaine is trying to jog Fred's memory of their conversation at the party by recounting what she told him.
jokes.punchline: The story itself — Oswald's casual 'I'm gonna go catch a movie' after being told JFK was shot — is a delightfully absurd piece of dark comedy, delivered as a totally sincere memory-jog.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's better jokes. The content of Elaine's party anecdote is itself darkly funny — Oswald's breezy alibi is a great absurdist invention — and it's doubly funny because this is the conversation Elaine expects Fred to remember. The joke works on multiple levels: the absurdity of the Oswald story, and the meta-absurdity of Elaine thinking THIS is a memorable conversation opener.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I got a commercial! It's for a coffee table book about coffee tables. You put your coffee on it, and it's a book, so you can read about coffee while you drink your coffee from the coffee table that's also a book.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's the dumbest thing I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, no! It's genius! And I'm gonna be in commercials for Leapin' Larry's!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Leapin' Larry's? What is that?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's a restaurant!

jokes.text: I remember someone had played tic-tac-toe on the bathroom door and the X's won. They went diagonally from the top left to the bottom right.
jokes.setup: Elaine recounts a vivid story (Oswald connection, JFK assassination) to jog Fred's memory of their conversation in front of the bathroom door.
jokes.punchline: Fred remembers the tic-tac-toe game on the bathroom door in precise detail — completely ignoring the historic assassination anecdote — revealing him as someone who registers only the utterly trivial.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best jokes. The joke has a beautiful structure: Elaine deploys her most memorable-possible story (JFK assassination, Oswald's wink) and Fred filters it out entirely, remembering only the tic-tac-toe diagonal on the bathroom door — described with the precision he denied to everything else. This is exceptional character comedy that also characterizes Elaine as someone who generates memorable experiences that still somehow don't stick. The specificity ('diagonally from the top left to the bottom right') is what makes it sing.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, I've been listening to this scanner, and I realize something... we're all just watching the watchers.

jokes.text: I'm watching the watchers, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Kramer explains he bought a police/emergency scanner that picks up everything — fires, harbor patrol, police.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm watching the watchers' — delivered with complete Kramer self-satisfaction, as if this is a profound act of civic vigilance rather than a quirky hobby.
jokes.explanation: Very quotable. Kramer's grandiose self-mythologizing is perfectly compressed into four words. The gap between the mundane reality (listening to a scanner) and the self-appointed importance ('watching the watchers') is pure Kramer. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, because being a fireman is really a cushy job. I mean, what could be more relaxing than running into burning buildings?

jokes.text: Yeah, those civil servants who risk their lives really got it made.
jokes.setup: Kramer wistfully says he wishes he'd become a fireman.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's deadpan sarcasm — 'really got it made' — punctures Kramer's romanticism about dangerous public service work.
jokes.explanation: Functional Jerry sarcasm. Does its job efficiently. Not particularly original but well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're not even qualified to steer the front of a car.

jokes.text: You're lucky they let you drive a car.
jokes.setup: Kramer romantically describes his childhood dream of steering the back of a hook-and-ladder fire truck.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dismissive 'You're lucky they let you drive a car' — implying Kramer's competence doesn't clear even basic automotive standards, let alone emergency vehicles.
jokes.explanation: Good Jerry-on-Kramer put-down. Clean structure and excellent economy. Funny in context because we know Kramer IS chaotic and probably shouldn't be trusted behind the wheel — which pays off gloriously later in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, turn left here! No, no, your other left! You're going the wrong way!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Watch out for that pothole! Come on, people, I've seen better driving at the Shady Pines Retirement Community!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Step on it! You're going to miss the light!

jokes.text: No, no, no. They're taking the West Side Highway. At this time of day? That's insane. They're heading straight into gridlock. Oh, those fools.
jokes.setup: Kramer is listening to the police/fire scanner and hears emergency vehicles' routes.
jokes.punchline: Kramer is backseat-driving EMERGENCY VEHICLES via scanner, calling professional first responders 'fools' for their route choices.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Kramer joke. The premise — someone so convinced of their traffic knowledge that they armchair-quarterback life-or-death emergency routing — is perfectly Kramer. The escalation to 'Oh, those fools' lands particularly well. This also beautifully seeds the payoff when Kramer actually ends up on the fire truck. Strong character integration, original premise, good structure.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He doesn't even remember me.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, maybe you should take it as a compliment. I mean, how many women do you think he meets in a week? You'd think he'd remember all of them.

jokes.text: Really? I'm surprised. He doesn't meet that many women.
jokes.setup: Elaine says Fred remembers every detail of the night except her.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's observation that Fred doesn't meet many women — implying remembering Elaine should have been easy given the scarcity — is a subtle but precise insult to Fred.
jokes.explanation: Good observational character joke. Economical and precise. The implication — Fred is pathetic enough that remembering ONE woman should've been easy — is understated and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: We shouldn't keep secrets from each other.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You're right. Well, I have something to tell you. I'm not really a marine biologist.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: What? George, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, I know. But sometimes I just say things, and people believe me. I don't know why.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: This is unbelievable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: There's more. I'm going through some... changes. You know, like a woman does once a month.

jokes.text: I don't know anything about your cycles.
jokes.setup: Susan pushes George to share secrets as a sign of relationship trust. George deflects by claiming she has secrets from him too.
jokes.punchline: George's example of a secret Susan keeps from him: her menstrual cycle — which he describes not with embarrassment but with genuine bewilderment, as if it's classified intelligence.
jokes.explanation: Very George. The deflection is clumsy and socially inappropriate, revealing George's emotional intelligence gap perfectly. Treating a normal biological reality as a 'secret' on par with an ATM code is a great George-ism. Character integration is the clear strength here.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I have something I need to tell you.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: What is it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My ATM code is Bosco.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Bosco? Why Bosco?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Because I like saying it. Bosco.

jokes.text: Elaine's dread about Peterman's dinner invitation: 'I can't sit with him. He tells these stories. It's gonna be awful.' Jerry: 'Yeah, sounds like fun.'
jokes.setup: Elaine is dreading a dinner with J. Peterman who apparently tells insufferable stories, and begs Jerry to come.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's 'Yeah, sounds like fun' — cheerful acceptance delivered with total awareness that he's agreeing to suffer.
jokes.explanation: Competent ironic acceptance from Jerry. Gets the point across efficiently but doesn't add much beyond what's obvious. The setup for the Peterman dinner is doing more work than the joke itself.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What happened to your leg?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, this? Prosthetic. But don't worry, they still call me Leapin' Larry.

jokes.text: Well, from now on, I'll keep you apprised of my cycles.
jokes.setup: George complains he doesn't know anything about Susan's cycles.
jokes.punchline: Susan's dry, slightly threatening 'I'll keep you apprised of my cycles' — the perfect comeback that calls George's bluff and promises more TMI than he bargained for.
jokes.explanation: Clean, economical comeback. 'Apprised of my cycles' is the perfect formal/clinical phrasing to maximize the awkwardness. Well-structured as a direct reversal of George's tactic.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: My whole left side's asleep.

dialogue.speaker: Larry David
dialogue.line: Well, shake it out, man.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I can't feel my leg.

jokes.text: Please. / Anything else? / We're out of Bosco.
jokes.setup: Susan offers to update George on her cycles. George says 'Please' (meaning 'please don't'). Susan asks 'Anything else?' as if taking requests.
jokes.punchline: George's non sequitur 'We're out of Bosco' — pivoting from intimate conversation to a grocery concern — inadvertently reveals his ATM code (Bosco) to the audience while seeming to be a mundane topic change.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's master structural joke. George's mundane 'We're out of Bosco' simultaneously: (1) gets a laugh as absurd non sequitur deflection, (2) reveals to the audience (not Susan) that his secret ATM code IS Bosco — the chocolate syrup — which pays off in enormous callbacks throughout the episode. The genius is that it hides in plain sight as a throwaway grocery comment. The secret-within-a-scene structure is exceptional writing. This is comparable in craft to great Seinfeld structural jokes — deserves high craft score.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What's wrong with your leg?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh, it's nothing, I just tweaked it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's a great limp. How'd you do that?

jokes.text: My foot's asleep again.
jokes.setup: Jerry is meeting Leapin' Larry, a one-legged appliance store owner, for the first time, in the middle of a professional conversation.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's foot falls asleep again — directly in front of a man with a prosthetic leg — setting up the imminent disaster.
jokes.explanation: The running gag payoff is structurally satisfying. The audience knows where this is going (Jerry will limp, Leapin' Larry will take offense) and the dread-anticipation is funny. A good setup for the cringe sequence to follow.

dialogue.speaker: Larry David
dialogue.line: You're making fun of me? You're mocking me?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm not making fun of you. Why don't you laugh at yourself? Everybody else does.

jokes.text: That is a great impression. [Jerry visibly limping due to his asleep foot in front of Leapin' Larry]
jokes.setup: Jerry's foot fell asleep and he's walking awkwardly; Leapin' Larry, who has a prosthetic leg, watches.
jokes.punchline: Larry says 'That is a great impression' — meaning he thinks Jerry is mocking his gait — while Jerry is genuinely physically incapacitated. A perfect cringe misunderstanding.
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe payoff. The structure has been built across three scenes — foot falls asleep, prosthetic leg is revealed, Jerry limps again — and 'That is a great impression' is the perfect, devastating punchline. The audience has been dreading exactly this moment. Well-executed cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, Kramer, you really pulled a munjamba on that audition.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: A munjamba? What's a munjamba?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Exactly. Nobody knows. That's the whole point. You were so bad, there's no word for it.

jokes.text: I just came from Leapin' Larry's. What, making fun of crippled people? Is that what you've sunk to?
jokes.setup: George sees Jerry limping out of the store and confronts him.
jokes.punchline: George — of all people — takes the moral high ground, appalled at the idea of mocking crippled people, unaware of the innocent explanation.
jokes.explanation: Solid scene transition joke. George's sanctimony is funny given his own character, but the joke is mostly functional — it's setting up the next beat rather than delivering an independent laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I got a great sense of direction. I can navigate anywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's impressive, but you don't really go anywhere.

jokes.text: No, I didn't do it on purpose. My foot fell asleep.
jokes.setup: George accuses Jerry of mocking a crippled person.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's completely straight defense — 'My foot fell asleep' — which is true but sounds utterly implausible as an excuse.
jokes.explanation: Good running gag continuation. The defense is true but sounds like a terrible lie, which is very Seinfeld — innocent behavior misread as monstrous.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know if I can do this, Jerry. What if I'm not 'on'? What if I go in there and I'm 'off'?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you're definitely 'off'.

jokes.text: You know, the guy has one leg and he still calls himself Leapin' Larry. You'd think he'd have a sense of humour.
jokes.setup: Larry exploded in anger at Jerry for the apparent mockery.
jokes.punchline: George points out the self-deprecating humor implicit in 'Leapin' Larry' as a name for a one-legged man, concluding Larry has no right to be humorless.
jokes.explanation: Sharp George logic. The observation is actually correct — 'Leapin' Larry' IS inherently self-deprecating for a one-legged man — but the conclusion (he should therefore accept mockery) is the flawed George-ism. Good dark comedy with solid character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Costanza
dialogue.line: I can't even sew a button on a shirt. My wife, she does all that stuff. I'm helpless.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Costanza
dialogue.line: I don't know how to do anything. I can't cook, I can't clean... I'm like a child.

jokes.text: Kramer: 'You know, the guy has one leg and he still calls himself Leapin' Larry. You'd think he'd have a sense of humour.'
jokes.setup: Kramer has lost the commercial because Larry thought he was mocking his disability.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's logic: Larry chose the name 'Leapin'' while having one leg, which Kramer reads as self-deprecating humor — so why is Larry upset now?
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy delivered with Kramer's complete sincerity. The logic is both terrible and somehow coherent — Leapin' Larry IS a funny name for someone with one leg, and Kramer's expectation of reciprocal humor is deranged but internally consistent. 'You'd think he'd have a sense of humour' as the conclusion is perfectly calibrated self-righteous Kramer. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Fred
dialogue.line: Do you have other plans for dinner?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, but I'll try to remember to show up this time.

jokes.text: You just joked yourself out of that commercial, didn't you, munjamba?
jokes.setup: Jerry has just lost the Leapin' Larry commercial deal due to the misunderstanding.
jokes.punchline: 'Didn't you, munjamba?' — George's use of 'munjamba' as an affectionate/mocking nickname is a perfect George-ism, absurd and condescending simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: The word 'munjamba' is doing all the heavy lifting and it works beautifully — an invented word that sounds both affectionate and dismissive, used with complete George confidence. This is the kind of verbal specificity that elevates Seinfeld above most sitcoms.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So the embassy is closed because of a bomb threat...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A bomb threat? What am I supposed to do?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you need to get your code from inside the embassy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: How am I supposed to get in if it's closed?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You could call them, explain the situation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Jerry, there's a bomb threat! They're not taking calls about access codes!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're just going to leave your code in there?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What else can I do? It's a bomb threat!

jokes.text: They just don't know what street to take. Remember I got us to Yankee Stadium in rush hour in 15 minutes? It's all up here, Jerry. All up here. It's innate.
jokes.setup: Kramer sees on TV the fire he was monitoring and continues to backseat-drive the emergency response.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's completely unsolicited testimony to his own traffic genius — 'It's innate' — as if his ability to navigate to Yankee Stadium qualifies him to direct emergency services.
jokes.explanation: Consistent, strong Kramer character comedy. The self-mythologizing is pitch-perfect. 'It's innate' is a very quotable Kramer line. This is important plot setup for the payoff scene.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: I'm J. Peterman.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: J. Crew?

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: Peterman.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh, I thought you said J. Crew.

jokes.text: The amazing thing is you never have any place to go.
jokes.setup: Kramer brags about his traffic navigation abilities and intimate knowledge of the city.
jokes.punchline: Jerry deflates the self-importance: Kramer's superpower is useless because he never actually goes anywhere.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jerry-on-Kramer one-liner. Sharp, economical, and captures a genuine Kramer truth — he has all this energy and competence that goes nowhere because he has no actual goals. Very good economy (one sentence, perfect irony).

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: A triumvirate of gentlemen.

jokes.text: Nobody mentioned anything about Peterman. / If I did, would you have gone? / No way. / There you go.
jokes.setup: George realizes he's being taken to dinner with J. Peterman, whom he doesn't know.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's casual 'There you go' — confirming he deliberately withheld Peterman's presence — is a perfect expression of Jerry's amoral social maneuvering.
jokes.explanation: Good back-and-forth. Jerry's complicity is revealed with perfect economy. 'There you go' is a masterclass in acknowledging bad behavior without remorse.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't go to dinner tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I have to... wash my car.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You don't have a car.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: I don't like being on, Jerry. I'd much rather be off. / Trust me, you're off.
jokes.setup: George complains he'll have to 'be on' all night with Peterman — meaning he'll have to perform socially.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's 'Trust me, you're off' — agreeing with George but flipping the meaning: George is never 'on' socially regardless of his intentions.
jokes.explanation: Superb wordplay built on the double meaning of 'on/off.' George uses it to mean 'performing/not performing.' Jerry repurposes it to mean 'charming/not charming.' Clean, fast, economical. One of the episode's better one-liners.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: Well, well, well. It appears we'll be dining together after all.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, about that...

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: I insist. Come. We have much to discuss.

jokes.text: I got this new shirt. The button fell off. Once the button falls off, that's it. I'll never fix it.
jokes.setup: Elaine runs into Fred again. He says he's depressed.
jokes.punchline: The source of Fred's depression: a button fell off his shirt. And his resigned certainty that he will NEVER fix it — a portrait of profound learned helplessness.
jokes.explanation: Very funny character moment for Fred. The specificity of 'I'll never fix it' — not 'I haven't fixed it yet' but a permanent declaration — is what makes this sing. Fred is established as someone profoundly defeated by minor inconveniences, which is a fresh comic character type.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: I was in Morocco, sipping mint tea at a café overlooking the medina. A woman approached me—eyes like desert stars. We spent the evening talking, dancing, falling in love under the Saharan sky. By dawn, we both knew this was destiny. She whispered, 'Will you stay with me forever?' I said, 'Of course.' Then she said, 'Great, because we're opening a Banana Republic in Casablanca and we need an operations manager.'

jokes.text: They make you nauseous, right? Yeah. Yeah, that's right. You remembered.
jokes.setup: Fred says he's going to get vitamins because he feels depleted. Elaine says she never takes them.
jokes.punchline: Fred remembers that vitamins make Elaine nauseous — a tiny, trivial fact — when he couldn't remember a single thing about their extended conversation at a party, including a JFK assassination story. His memory is selectively useless.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback and character consistency. Fred remembers one tiny mundane detail (vitamins = nausea) but forgot an entire 10-minute conversation about the Kennedy assassination. His selective memory is established as a consistent character trait, and the irony works perfectly. 'You remembered' lands as both a payoff and an indictment.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Mr. Peterman, I really should get back to the office.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: Nonsense! I'll drive you. You know, this reminds me of the time I was in Morocco...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's okay, I can take a cab.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: A cab? George, a cab is just a vehicle. But a ride with me? That's a journey. I was in Marrakech, 1987, with a woman named Svetlana...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Mr. Peterman—

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: She had eyes like the Mediterranean. We drove through the desert for three days. And the things we saw, George. The things we saw.

jokes.text: All right. You're locked up in a prison in Turkey. I have your wallet. The only way I can bribe the guards to get you out is for you to give me your ATM code. / Call the embassy. / They're closed. / Why? / Bomb threat. / We're in Turkey? / Midnight Express, my friend. / My card won't work. They're not on the PLUS system.
jokes.setup: Jerry challenges George's claim that he'd give the code only if someone's life depended on it, constructing an elaborate scenario.
jokes.punchline: George pokes holes in every element of Jerry's scenario, culminating in 'My card won't work. They're not on the PLUS system' — his most practical objection to an insane hypothetical.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top jokes. The escalating absurdist hypothetical is great, but the real genius is George's method of escape: rather than engaging emotionally or morally, he picks bureaucratic and logistical holes ('Bomb threat? They're not on the PLUS system'). 'Midnight Express, my friend' from Jerry is also a perfect line. The PLUS system punchline is exceptional character comedy — George defeats a life-or-death scenario with bank network incompatibility.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: Hello? Yes, this is he.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: What? No, no, no. You must have the wrong number.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: I was born in a small textile mill outside of Montreal. My mother was a seamstress, my father a loom operator. We had nothing but thread and dreams.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: What do you mean my house is on fire?!

jokes.text: J. Peterman. / J. Crew.
jokes.setup: Jerry introduces George to J. Peterman.
jokes.punchline: George, clearly nervous and unable to think of anything to say, blurts out 'J. Crew' — the competing catalogue brand — as his contribution to the introduction.
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly economical. Two words do enormous work: they capture George's social anxiety, his failure under pressure (he's already warned he might 'choke'), and the funniness of naming a competitor in response to someone's name. The parallel structure (J. Peterman / J. Crew) makes it feel inevitable in retrospect. This is what 'economy' looks like at its best.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I think? I think God doesn't want you to be happy. I think that's His whole thing. He's up there going, 'Oh, she likes him? Well, let's see how long that lasts.' And He makes the guy forget everything.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Is it? Is it really? Because every time you start to like someone, something goes wrong. It's like clockwork. It's like He's playing a game with you.

jokes.text: But not to worry. I'll tell the maître d' it'll just be the three bulls.
jokes.setup: Elaine has called to say she won't be joining them for dinner.
jokes.punchline: Peterman's response is 'the three bulls' — referring to himself, Jerry, and George in the most grandiosely masculine and absurd possible terms, as if they're big-game hunters.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Peterman character moment. 'The three bulls' is perfectly in the Peterman voice — grandiose, masculine, theatrical, absurd. It also makes the joke on Jerry and George, who are decidedly not bulls.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: Mother, I'm here. Your son is here. I've come to say goodbye to the woman who gave me life, who nurtured me, who believed in me when no one else would. I came as fast as I could, though the traffic on the FDR was absolutely Sophoclean in its tragedy.

jokes.text: What? He still wants to have dinner with us? Without Elaine? What for? / What is he, crazy? / Oh, we gotta get out of here. / Come on, weave your web, liar-man.
jokes.setup: Jerry and George realize Peterman still wants to dine, despite Elaine's absence.
jokes.punchline: 'Come on, weave your web, liar-man' — Jerry's immediate delegation of the escape excuse to George, with the affectionate/desperate nickname 'liar-man.'
jokes.explanation: Good panic-comedy scene. 'Liar-man' is a great coinage for George's supposed social skill — his ability to fabricate excuses — which then immediately fails ('I'm blank,' 'I'm choking'). Sets up the next joke.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm George Costanza. I'm, uh... a friend of Jerry's.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, actually I'm not really a friend. I'm more of an acquaintance. But Jerry asked me to come here, so...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't really know why. I mean, I don't know you. You don't know me. This is weird, right?

jokes.text: Come on, George, what's the matter with you? / I'm choking.
jokes.setup: Jerry urges George to come up with an excuse to avoid dinner with Peterman. George is famous as a schemer and liar.
jokes.punchline: George, the master manipulator, simply says 'I'm choking' — a frank, defeated admission that his skills have abandoned him entirely.
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment. The word 'choking' is borrowed from sports — a performer failing under pressure — which is both self-aware and completely George. The gap between his reputation as a schemer and his total helplessness here is funny.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The secret's killing me. I can't tell anybody.

dialogue.speaker: Dying Woman
dialogue.line: George, I'm dying.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, and it's killing me that I can't tell you.

jokes.text: Jerry's excuse to escape Peterman: 'Actually, I just remembered I promised this comedy club that I'd do a set tonight.' Peterman: 'I understand. No hard feelings.'
jokes.setup: Jerry needs an excuse to leave dinner with Peterman.
jokes.punchline: Jerry — a comedian — uses 'I have to do stand-up' as his excuse. It's both the most convenient excuse available and possibly true, making it meta and self-referential.
jokes.explanation: Lightly meta — Jerry Seinfeld using 'I have to do stand-up' as a sitcom excuse. Works on multiple levels without being heavy-handed about it. The real joke is George being left behind.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My PIN is 'Bosco'.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Bosco? What kind of PIN is Bosco?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's a chocolate syrup! You remember Bosco? They had the ads on TV when we were kids.

jokes.text: George and I will miss your company. Fong, it will just be two this evening. / Well, George... we dine.
jokes.setup: Jerry successfully makes an excuse (a comedy club set) and escapes. George cannot.
jokes.punchline: Peterman's aristocratic 'Well, George... we dine' — treating a restaurant dinner as a grand occasion, delivered to a visibly trapped George.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Peterman line. 'We dine' — not 'we're having dinner' — is perfectly in character. The theatrical gravitas applied to a simple meal, and George's trapped-animal situation, makes this a satisfying cringe setup.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, no! Don't say it! Please don't say it!

dialogue.speaker: Peterman's mother
dialogue.line: Bosco...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No! Why would you say that?!

jokes.text: I can't believe this. This guy's standing me up?
jokes.setup: Elaine agreed to a date with Fred, who doesn't even remember her. She's waiting and he hasn't arrived.
jokes.punchline: The irony: Elaine is being stood up by a man she doesn't even like, who can't remember her, whose existence annoys her — and she's OFFENDED he's not there.
jokes.explanation: Good character irony. Elaine being stood up by someone she's only dating because he forgot her is a nice ironic loop. The reaction beat lands.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My brain is on fire. There's nothing there. It's like looking into the sun. I can't even think of a lie. My mind is completely blank.

jokes.text: And there, tucked into the river's bend, was the object of my search... the Guangia River Market. Fabrics and spices traded under a starlit sky. It was there that I discovered the Pamplona Beret. Sizes seven and a half through eight and three quarters. Price, $35.
jokes.setup: Peterman is telling George an exotic adventure story over dinner.
jokes.punchline: After the lush, romantic travelogue prose about fabrics and spices under a starlit sky, the sudden clinical specificity: 'Sizes seven and a half through eight and three quarters. Price, $35.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes, and quintessentially Peterman. The joke is entirely structural: extended purple prose travelogue crashes into dry catalogue copy ('Price, $35'). This is excellent character comedy — only Peterman could deliver this — and it perfectly lampoons the real J. Peterman catalogue's overwritten style. The structure is immaculate: long romantic buildup, abrupt commercial puncture.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your PIN is your birthday, right?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your mother's birthday?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Let me think... what would George Costanza pick as a secret code? Something that makes him feel good about himself... Giddy up!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Is it 'It's not a lie if you believe it'?

jokes.text: It's fourth and inches and the Giants are going for it. You've gotta love sports.
jokes.setup: Peterman is riding with George and apparently watching something out the window.
jokes.punchline: Peterman, the exotic adventure catalogue man, suddenly commenting on American football like a regular guy — the jarring tonal shift underscores his unknowability.
jokes.explanation: Interesting Peterman character joke — the sudden banality contrasts his usual exoticism. 'You've gotta love sports' is an aggressively average thing for this man to say. Moderately effective.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm like a junkie. I need my chocolate fix. Every day, same time, I'm jonesing for a Snickers bar.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My dealer is the guy at the corner bodega. He knows what I want before I even walk in.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I've hit rock bottom. I'm eating chocolate chips straight from the bag at two in the morning.

jokes.text: Peterman here. / What? Oh, no. All right. I'll be right there. / It's my mother. She's at death's door. I just pray to God we can make it there in time.
jokes.setup: George is trapped in the car with Peterman going to dinner.
jokes.punchline: A phone call suddenly transforms the dinner into a life-or-death race to a dying mother's bedside — and George is along for the ride.
jokes.explanation: Good plot escalation. The joke is mostly structural — George is being dragged from a dinner he didn't want to a deathbed scene he definitely didn't want. 'Death's door' from Peterman, even in genuine distress, sounds theatrical because it's Peterman. The comedy is in George's helplessness.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not a lie if you believe it.

jokes.text: Peterman receives a call — his mother is 'at death's door' and he must go immediately. He tells George 'I still have George here to help me through this.'
jokes.setup: George has been trapped at dinner with Peterman. Peterman gets a call about his dying mother.
jokes.punchline: Rather than releasing George, Peterman conscripts him into grief support: 'Fortunately, I still have George here to help me through this.' George cannot escape.
jokes.explanation: George's escalating entrapment is a beautifully sustained cringe. The logic that a family emergency means George must stay (rather than go) is Peterman's unassailable self-absorption. 'Fortunately, I still have George' frames George as a resource, not a human. George's fate is sealed.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: Her last word... it had to mean something. It had to be profound.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: I've spent years trying to unlock its meaning. Was it a revelation? A warning? A final piece of wisdom?

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: I consulted philosophers, theologians, even a medium.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: And then I discovered the truth. It was someone's ATM code.

jokes.text: You're attracted to him because he can't remember you. / I am? But that's so sick. / That's God's plan. He doesn't really want anyone to get together.
jokes.setup: Elaine realizes she's upset about being stood up by Fred, a man she doesn't even like.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's explanation: she's attracted to Fred BECAUSE he can't remember her — reverse psychology of attraction. Then the punchline escalates to theology: 'That's God's plan. He doesn't really want anyone to get together.'
jokes.explanation: Strong observational joke with an absurd escalation. The first part (attraction to someone who forgets you) is a sharp psychological observation. The pivot to 'That's God's plan / He doesn't really want anyone to get together' is a fantastic Jerry-ism — cosmic cynicism delivered as casual wisdom. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I have to go. My... my cat is sick.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I gotta go too. My... my mother is expecting me. She's making pork chops.

jokes.text: I'm George Costanza. I was having dinner with your son.
jokes.setup: George finds himself in a hospital room with Peterman's dying mother, whom he has never met. Peterman has briefly stepped out.
jokes.punchline: George introduces himself to a dying woman with his full name and social context — as if she might know someone named Costanza. The mundanity of the explanation in the face of death is deeply George.
jokes.explanation: Very funny George moment. The formal self-introduction to someone at death's door — 'I was having dinner with your son' — is absurdly social in a context that requires no such accounting. Pure George: defaulting to social convention when emotionally lost.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: Now theorizing Bosco was my mother's secret lover

jokes.text: I really should be getting back to my fiancée. We had this big fight yesterday... and, well, she wants to know my secret code. I don't know. I can't tell her. The funny thing is, you know, I would really love to tell someone... because it's killing me.
jokes.setup: George is left alone with Peterman's comatose/near-death mother and told to 'just talk to her.'
jokes.punchline: George, unable to make meaningful small talk with a dying woman, defaults to his actual obsession: the ATM code drama, confessing his secret to someone who literally cannot judge or tell anyone.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural setup. George's self-absorption is so complete that he turns a dying woman's bedside into a therapy session about his ATM code. The logic is perfect George: she's the ideal confessor because she can't tell anyone. This is where the episode's climactic joke is planted.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You must have learned something from that commercial disaster.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Nope. Not a thing.

jokes.text: You wanna know what it is? It's Bosco. You know, the chocolate syrup. I love that stuff. I pour it in milk. It's my favourite drink. Boy, that is a relief. Bosco.
jokes.setup: George, confessing his secret to a dying woman, finally reveals his ATM code.
jokes.punchline: The code is 'Bosco' — the chocolate syrup — which the audience already knows from the earlier non sequitur. The payoff is the word itself: it's funny that a grown man's deepest secret is his love of chocolate milk syrup.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's primary payoff and it earns every point. The code is revealed to be Bosco — which the audience was already told via the planted non sequitur earlier — confirming the plant was intentional. The word 'Bosco' is inherently funny, the choice is perfectly George (childlike sweet tooth, total vulnerability in its silliness), and the structure of confessing to a dying woman is both absurd and oddly moving. 'Boy, that is a relief' seals it — George has been tortured by keeping this, and the secret is... Bosco. Extremely high quotability and rewatch value.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Chief, we can shave three minutes off response time if we take this shortcut through the park.

dialogue.speaker: Fire Chief
dialogue.line: We can't go through the park, Kramer. There are regulations.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Regulations? What good are regulations if people are burning?

dialogue.speaker: Fire Chief
dialogue.line: The regulations are there for a reason.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, to make sure nobody saves anybody!

jokes.text: George's audible relief after telling the secret: 'Boy, that is a relief. Bosco.'
jokes.setup: George has just told his secret code to a dying stranger.
jokes.punchline: 'Boy, that is a relief.' — George experiences genuine catharsis from confessing to someone who is unconscious and dying. Then he murmurs 'Bosco' again, savoring the relief.
jokes.explanation: The button on the confession. George needed to tell someone so badly that telling a comatose woman is genuinely cathartic. The second 'Bosco' murmured in relief is the perfect final exhale. George's self-absorption is at its most absurdly pure here.

dialogue.speaker: Radio
dialogue.line: We have a fire call coming in for Larry's store on 5th and Broadway.

dialogue.speaker: Fire Captain
dialogue.line: Alright, we're on our way.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait! I know a shortcut! You take 5th to the park, cut through the park, come out on Central Park West...

dialogue.speaker: Fire Captain
dialogue.line: We know the way.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, no, this is better! Trust me on this!

jokes.text: Bosco. / Mama? / Quiet, quiet. It's a secret. / Bosco! Bosco! / Shut up. It's a secret. / Mama, what are you trying to say? / Bosco.
jokes.setup: George has just confessed his secret code 'Bosco' to Peterman's dying mother, thinking she can't respond.
jokes.punchline: She CAN respond. She starts shouting 'Bosco!' deliriously while George desperately tries to shush her and Peterman rushes in trying to interpret his mother's dying words.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic peak. The dying woman shouting 'Bosco!' while George frantically hushes her ('Shut up. It's a secret.') is perfectly constructed escalating cringe. The layers work: George chose a dying person as his confessor precisely because she couldn't tell anyone, and she immediately shouts it. Peterman trying to find meaning in his dying mother's final word ('Bosco') adds a tragic-comic dimension. The scene is a masterwork of escalation and structural payoff. This is among the best sequences in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. I'm the problem here. I'm the one who's crazy. I'm sitting here obsessing over some guy named Fred who I don't even know, making up scenarios in my head about what he's thinking. This is insane. I'm insane.

jokes.text: Peterman's mother, supposedly near death and speechless, suddenly says 'Bosco' — then louder as George desperately tries to silence her — then her final word before dying is 'Bosco.'
jokes.setup: George has just secretly told Peterman's mother his ATM code, assuming she can't hear or repeat it.
jokes.punchline: She repeats 'Bosco' — loudly, to her son. George tries to silence her. She says it again. Then she dies, with 'Bosco' as her final word.
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic peak and one of Seinfeld's greatest set pieces. The structure is flawless: George confesses to a dying woman assuming she can't hear, she promptly begins announcing it, he tries to shush a dying woman about his chocolate syrup password, and her final word to her son is 'Bosco.' The dark comedy of George's priorities (stopping the PIN leak while his companion is literally dying) is perfectly calibrated. The escalation through each 'Bosco' is exceptional. This is a top-tier Seinfeld moment.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Listen up, we got a job to do. This is Cosmo Kramer, coming to you live from the back of the fire truck. We are en route to a structure fire at 129 West 81st Street. Everybody stay calm, we know what we're doing.

jokes.text: Peterman's first words after his mother dies: 'Bosco?'
jokes.setup: Mama Peterman has died, her final word being 'Bosco.'
jokes.punchline: Peterman repeats it as a question — to him it's a mysterious dying wish or legacy message. The audience knows it's George's ATM code. The gap between Peterman's interpretation and reality is the joke.
jokes.explanation: One word, maximum comedic devastation. 'Bosco?' with Peterman's bewildered gravity — treating a dying woman's utterance of a chocolate brand as a profound legacy message — is the perfect button. The economy is unmatched: the entire irony of the scene compressed into a single confused question.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're trapped by the ATM machine during a fire?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't get out! The keypad is locked!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What's the code?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't tell you the code!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, the building is on fire!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Nobody gets my code. That's my code!

jokes.text: This never would have happened if you hadn't bailed out on me. / I did not bail out on you. / Why couldn't you include me in your excuse?
jokes.setup: George is furious that Jerry escaped the Peterman dinner, leaving George to spend the night with a dying woman.
jokes.punchline: George's complaint isn't about the death or trauma — it's that Jerry didn't include him in the fake excuse. Pure Seinfeld moral inversion.
jokes.explanation: Good Seinfeld moral universe joke. George's grievance skips past the actual trauma (watching a woman die) to the procedural complaint (not being included in the lie). Perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You want some chocolate syrup?

jokes.text: Jerry's Sherlock Holmes-style deduction of George's ATM code: 'Well, we can throw out birthdays immediately. That's too obvious. And no numbers for you. You're a word man.'
jokes.setup: George bets Jerry can't guess his ATM code. Jerry accepts.
jokes.punchline: Jerry launches into a full psychological profile as if cracking a criminal's mind — 'you're a word man' — treating George's PIN selection as a deep character study.
jokes.explanation: Jerry's deduction scene is a comedic tour de force. 'You're a word man' is a great line. This sets up the extended roast of George that follows and the eventual almost-correct conclusion.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Listen to this: 'The Beekeeper'. A man stands alone on a vast desert plateau. His eyes narrow as he gazes upon the horizon. He has no name. He has no earthly possessions. But he has a dream. He has the Nina, the Pinta, and the Santa Maria.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's me! That's the Beekeeper!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's not you.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It has to be me! Look, I'm a beekeeper!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, no. It says here: 'In 1952, he murdered his mother.'

jokes.text: Well, you're weak, spineless. A man of temptations. But what tempts you? You're a portly fellow, a little long in the waistband. So, what's your pleasure? Is it the salty snacks you crave? No, no, no. Yours is a sweet tooth. Oh, you may stray...but you'll always return to your dark master...the cocoa bean.
jokes.setup: Jerry claims he can guess George's ATM code and begins an extended, theatrical deduction.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's Sherlock Holmes-style deduction — 'weak, spineless, a man of temptations...portly...long in the waistband...your dark master...the cocoa bean' — is both brilliantly in character and hilariously on-the-nose.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best extended comedy sequences. Jerry's deduction plays like a parody of Sherlock Holmes — the theatrical, confident build from character assessment to specific culinary vice — and the phrase 'your dark master, the cocoa bean' is genuinely inspired. The escalation is beautifully paced. This sequence also dramatically raises the tension because the audience knows Jerry is RIGHT. 'Portly fellow, a little long in the waistband' is particularly quotable.

jokes.text: And only the purest syrup nectar can satisfy you. / I gotta go. / If you could, you'd guzzle it by the gallon. / Ovaltine, Hershey's, Nestlé's Quik. / Shut up! Shut up!
jokes.setup: Jerry's deduction is landing close to home; George is fleeing but Jerry keeps going.
jokes.punchline: Jerry listing competing chocolate products ('Ovaltine, Hershey's, Nestlé's Quik') as the thing George rejects in favor of Bosco, while George screams 'Shut up!' — perfectly escalating George's panic as Jerry closes in.
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the deduction scene. George's 'Shut up! Shut up!' under siege from chocolate brand names is both funny and shows how close to home Jerry is getting. The listing of brands (Ovaltine, Hershey's, Nestlé's Quik) as decoys rejected for Bosco is a perfect comedic list.

jokes.text: You just checked your watch. Are you thinking of bailing?
jokes.setup: They are at the funeral for Peterman's mother. George, still traumatized by the deduction scene, checks his watch.
jokes.punchline: Jerry catching George checking his watch and immediately assuming 'bailing' — a callback to George's whole escape problem.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke that acknowledges the recurring 'escape' theme. Not especially memorable but does its job.

jokes.text: When Mama said 'Bosco'...she must have been communicating something. Her legacy. A dying wish, perhaps. Mothers say things.
jokes.setup: At the funeral, Peterman is still trying to decode his mother's dying word 'Bosco.'
jokes.punchline: Peterman's solemn 'Mothers say things' — as profound and useless a statement as has ever been uttered — while the audience knows the 'dying wish' is George's ATM code.
jokes.explanation: Great continuation of the Bosco arc. Peterman's theatrical grieving and his need to find meaning in 'Bosco' — while George stands there knowing the truth — creates beautiful dramatic irony. 'Mothers say things' as a profound conclusion is a perfect Peterman non-statement.

jokes.text: I'm speaking at a woman's rights conference. / Yes, and I'm speaking at a men's conference.
jokes.setup: Elaine and George are simultaneously trying to escape Peterman at the funeral.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's excuse is a women's rights conference; George immediately claims a men's conference — a made-up institution that exists only to parallel Elaine's excuse, exposing both as lying simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: Funny escalation of the escape theme. The 'men's conference' as George's improvised symmetrical lie is absurd and perfectly George — he's so desperate he just mirrors whatever's available. Peterman's 'I don't believe that for a minute' nails it.

jokes.text: I don't believe that for a minute.
jokes.setup: George claims he has to leave for a men's conference.
jokes.punchline: Peterman's flat 'I don't believe that for a minute' — seeing through the lie instantly, but choosing to be polite about it — yet another moment of George's schemes failing to work.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Peterman line. Maximum economy — seven words communicate intelligence, politeness, and total see-through of George's lie. The fact that he then continues to trap George anyway is the real punch.

jokes.text: Fortunately, I still have George here to help me through this.
jokes.setup: Both Jerry and Elaine have escaped. George is still trapped.
jokes.punchline: Peterman's 'Fortunately, I still have George' — delivered as if George's presence is a gift rather than a trap — while George is clearly a prisoner of circumstance.
jokes.explanation: Good cringe payoff. The irony of Peterman's gratitude while George is trapped is satisfying. Not as sharp as some earlier jokes but effective.

jokes.text: Peterman tells George: 'You know, George, growing up as a boy in Costa Rica...I heard a rumour that Mama had taken a lover. Perhaps Bosco was this man's name.'
jokes.setup: Peterman is still processing his mother's dying word while George — trapped again — must listen.
jokes.punchline: Peterman speculates that 'Bosco' was the name of his mother's secret lover in Costa Rica — an elaborate romantic backstory invented for what is actually an ATM code.
jokes.explanation: The Bosco joke achieves new heights. Now 'Bosco' is a Costa Rican lover's name in Peterman's romantic mythology. The specificity of 'growing up as a boy in Costa Rica' grounds the absurdity perfectly. George must stand there knowing the truth while Peterman invents this lush backstory. Exceptional Peterman characterization — only he would spin an ATM code into a telenovela.

jokes.text: You wanna come to the fire station with me? / Fire station? / Yeah, I made a map of my shortcuts. I'm gonna rock their world.
jokes.setup: Kramer has been backseat-driving emergency vehicles via scanner for the whole episode.
jokes.punchline: Kramer has created a MAP of his shortcuts to present to the fire department — escalating his presumption from passive critique to an unsolicited in-person presentation. 'I'm gonna rock their world.'
jokes.explanation: Great Kramer escalation. 'I'm gonna rock their world' — applied to a map of traffic shortcuts presented to professional firefighters — is perfect Kramer grandiosity. His confidence is the joke.

jokes.text: No, I didn't.
jokes.setup: Jerry tells Jerry that the important thing is that Jerry 'learned something' from the Leapin' Larry disaster.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's immediate, flat 'No, I didn't' — refusing the narrative arc of personal growth entirely.
jokes.explanation: Three words encapsulate Seinfeld's entire ethos: no lessons, no growth. This is the show's anti-sitcom DNA in pure form. 'No, I didn't' is a mission statement. The economy is as perfect as it gets.

jokes.text: Mr. Kramer, almost every week some hothead like yourself saunters in here talking about faster routes and snazzier colours for the truck. The fact is, we feel things are fine the way they are.
jokes.setup: Kramer has promised a 'magic show' of shortcuts.
jokes.punchline: The fire captain deflates Kramer with the revelation that this happens 'almost every week' — Kramer's uniqueness is a delusion — and 'snazzier colours for the truck' implies the range of civilian interference the department endures.
jokes.explanation: Great deflation. 'Almost every week some hothead like yourself' perfectly takes down Kramer's self-image — he's not a visionary, he's a recurring nuisance. 'Snazzier colours for the truck' is a perfect absurdist escalation of the types of civilian 'help' the fire department receives.

jokes.text: Attention, company 390. Structure fire at Leapin' Larry's Appliance Warehouse. Leapin' Larry's? Hey, that's uptown. You gotta take Amsterdam. / Stay out of this, Kramer.
jokes.setup: The fire at Leapin' Larry's is announced on the station scanner. Kramer is still at the station.
jokes.punchline: Kramer IMMEDIATELY tries to backseat-drive again — 'You gotta take Amsterdam' — now in person, to the actual firefighters, about the actual Leapin' Larry's fire. 'Stay out of this, Kramer.'
jokes.explanation: Excellent convergence of multiple storylines. Kramer's habit of backseat-driving emergency vehicles (established via scanner) now manifests IN PERSON at the fire station when Leapin' Larry's (the other storyline) burns down. The payoff of multiple planted jokes is very satisfying. 'Stay out of this, Kramer' from the captain is the perfect response.

jokes.text: Kramer somehow gets in/on the fire truck: 'Giddyup.'
jokes.setup: The fire trucks are dispatching to Leapin' Larry's. Kramer has been trying to insert himself into fire department operations.
jokes.punchline: 'Giddyup' — the single word Kramer says as he apparently commandeers or boards the fire truck. The cowboy/rodeo language applied to firefighting is absurdly Kramer.
jokes.explanation: One word that encapsulates everything about Kramer's character. 'Giddyup' suggests he's treating a fire truck like a horse — the reckless, joyful enthusiasm of a man who has absolutely no business operating emergency equipment. Maximum economy: 10/10. The word alone contains an entire physical performance.

jokes.text: Are you okay, cowboy? / Where do you need to go? / I drive the back of the truck.
jokes.setup: In the chaos of the fire truck launch, Kramer has apparently ended up on the fire truck.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's 'I drive the back of the truck' — referencing his childhood dream from earlier in the episode, now being claimed as a credential in a real emergency situation.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding callback payoff. 'I drive the back of the truck' directly echoes Kramer's earlier childhood fantasy of steering the hook-and-ladder, which Jerry immediately dismissed ('You're lucky they let you drive a car'). Now Kramer IS on the truck, in a crisis, claiming this as a credential. The callback is both funny and structurally satisfying — an early throwaway line becomes the key to an absurd action-comedy climax.

jokes.text: We're gonna make a left onto Broadway. / No, I would advise against that. / Who is this? / It's Kramer.
jokes.setup: The fire truck is heading to Leapin' Larry's. Jerry is watching on a scanner/TV.
jokes.punchline: Kramer is on the fire truck, via radio, telling the captain not to take Broadway — still backseat-driving emergency vehicles, now from the vehicle itself. 'Who is this? It's Kramer.'
jokes.explanation: This is Kramer's arc reaching its logical absurd conclusion: he's gone from listening to the scanner at home and criticizing routes, to showing up at the fire station, to being ON the fire truck arguing routes with the captain IN PERSON. The escalation is perfectly constructed across the episode. 'Who is this? It's Kramer' is a great punctuation on the absurdity.

jokes.text: Leapin' Larry at the scene of his burning store: 'Where the hell is the fire department? I'm gonna lose the whole store.'
jokes.setup: Leapin' Larry's store is on fire. The fire truck is late because Kramer is steering it.
jokes.punchline: Larry is screaming for the fire department while the fire department's truck is being steered erratically by the man who just cost Larry his commercial — the ironic loop is complete.
jokes.explanation: The Seinfeld plot convergence at its finest — Kramer caused the delay in fire response by commandeering the truck, and now is at least partly responsible for Larry losing his store. The irony circle is complete and the escalation is devastating.

jokes.text: What are you doing, Kramer? You're all over the road. / Don't worry, cap, I can handle it.
jokes.setup: Kramer is actively operating the back of the fire truck, apparently driving it erratically.
jokes.punchline: 'Don't worry, cap, I can handle it' — the most alarming possible thing Kramer could say given his established track record, delivered with total confidence.
jokes.explanation: Functional escalation. 'Don't worry, cap, I can handle it' from Kramer — a man who was just told he shouldn't be there — is funny precisely because the audience knows he absolutely cannot handle it.

jokes.text: George, look. There's a man in there. Get out. You're in danger. / My sleeve, it's stuck in the machine. It ate my card. / George, give me your ATM card. / I don't have my ATM card. / George, you're obviously lying. Anyone can see that.
jokes.setup: The fire at Leapin' Larry's is raging; a man is trapped with his sleeve stuck in an ATM machine.
jokes.punchline: The emergency scenario converges perfectly with the episode's central joke: a man is literally trapped by an ATM, needing a card and a code — and George, who has been protecting his code all episode, is the one being asked.
jokes.explanation: This is the structural payoff of the entire episode's A-plot. The 'what if someone's life depended on it' scenario Jerry posited in the diner (dismissed as absurd) is now literally happening. George's 'I don't have my ATM card' and the immediate 'George, you're obviously lying. Anyone can see that' is a perfect beat. The scenario is exactly as contrived as Jerry's hypothetical — and it's actually happening.

jokes.text: Code! The code! / [George finally shouts his code in the emergency]
jokes.setup: George has been protecting his ATM code all episode. A man's life now depends on the code. The crowd is shouting 'Code! The code!'
jokes.punchline: George, the man who refused to tell his fiancée his code under any circumstances, is now being publicly pressured to shout it in a burning building. The dramatic irony — that the code is 'Bosco' — makes the climax both tense and inherently ridiculous.
jokes.explanation: Masterful structural payoff. The entire episode has been building to this: the ridiculous hypothetical scenario ('what if someone's life depended on it?') is now literally real, and George has to shout 'Bosco' in a burning building. The code itself — a childlike chocolate syrup — deflates the dramatic stakes perfectly. This is Seinfeld's signature trick: the mundane and the epic colliding.

jokes.text: The crowd at the fire scene chants 'Code! The code!' as George is pressured to reveal his ATM PIN to save the trapped man.
jokes.setup: George must shout his ATM code to unlock a machine trapping a man, surrounded by a crowd, during a fire.
jokes.punchline: A crowd of strangers chanting 'Code! The code!' — the episode's central secret becomes a public spectacle demanding George do the one thing he refused to do throughout.
jokes.explanation: The ultimate payoff of Jerry's 'if someone's life depended on it' exception. The very scenario George dismissed as paranoid fantasy has materialized: a person's life, a fire, fast cash needed. The crowd chanting 'Code! The code!' is the episode's structural climax. Everything — the code secrecy, the exception, the building fire, Kramer's fire truck — has converged on this moment. Exceptional construction.

jokes.text: Anyone for Bosco?
jokes.setup: The crisis is resolved. George has shouted his code. Everything is fine.
jokes.punchline: Jerry casually asking 'Anyone for Bosco?' — deploying the now-public secret as a casual snack offer, making the big dramatic reveal entirely mundane.
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode button. Three words do all the work: they dismiss the entire dramatic climax, confirm Jerry's knowledge of the code, and perfectly punctuate the episode's central joke. 'Anyone for Bosco?' is the most casual possible deployment of knowledge that cost George enormous stress. Maximum economy, perfect timing, and deeply quotable. This is what a great episode-closing joke looks like.

jokes.text: 'The Rogue's Wallet. It's where he kept his card, his dirty little secret. Short, devious, balding...his name was Costanza. He killed my mother.'
jokes.setup: The episode ends with the new J. Peterman catalogue arriving. Elaine reads the copy aloud.
jokes.punchline: Peterman has written George into his catalogue copy as a murderer ('He killed my mother') — his mother's dying word 'Bosco' has been romanticized into a sinister narrative about George Costanza, who 'killed my mother' by giving her the stress of shouting a secret code.
jokes.explanation: The episode's crowning achievement and its funniest moment. Every single thread is gathered: the Bosco secret, Peterman's mother's death, Peterman's overwritten catalogue copy, and George's culpability — all synthesized into a wallet description that accuses George Costanza of murder. 'Short, devious, balding...his name was Costanza. He killed my mother.' is a perfect comedy sentence. It's dark, absurd, structurally impeccable, deeply quotable, and rewards every minute of setup. The Peterman catalogue voice applied to George's ATM code is inspired. This is the joke that would be clipped on YouTube for decades. Comparable to the best Seinfeld episode-enders.

tmdb_overview: George dines with Elaine's boss; Jerry's sleeping foot costs him a commercial; Kramer gives traffic advice to firemen.
insight: Neurotic number-obsession drives 58 jokes in a mid-tier episode relying on character quirks over punchlines.
